clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man presents with a 3 cm firm, non-tender lateral neck mass that has been present for 6 weeks. He is a heavy smoker. There is no fever. Examination of the oral cavity and oropharynx is unremarkable. Which is the MOST appropriate first-line investigation?
options: CT scan of the neck with contrast
Open surgical biopsy under general anaesthesia
explanation: According to the neck mass lecture, ultrasound combined with FNAC is the recommended first-line investigation for a lateral neck lump in an adult, particularly when malignancy is suspected. Ultrasound characterises the lesion (solid vs cystic, node vs non-node) and guides the needle for cytology. A 6-week duration in a smoker over 40 years raises concern for metastatic SCC or lymphoma. CT is appropriate next for staging once cytology confirms malignancy, but it is not the first step. Open biopsy is the investigation of last resort because it may compromise subsequent surgical planes and carries a 3-10% false-positive risk if used in isolation. PET-CT is reserved for identifying an unknown primary after a negative clinical and endoscopic examination. FBC and ESR are supportive tests, not first-line diagnostic tools.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Ultrasound + FNAC is the recommended first-line investigation for a suspicious lateral neck mass in an adult smoker — it characterises the lesion and provides cytological diagnosis with minimal morbidity.
Incorrect: CT neck is used for staging and surgical planning once a tissue diagnosis is established; it is not the initial investigation of choice.
Incorrect: Open biopsy is the investigation of last resort; it should only be performed when all other modalities have failed to establish a diagnosis, as it may compromise surgical planes.
Incorrect: PET-CT is reserved for localising an occult primary tumour in a patient with confirmed metastatic neck disease and negative panendoscopy; it is not first-line.
Incorrect: FBC and ESR may support a diagnosis of lymphoma or infection but cannot characterise or diagnose a neck mass and are not the recommended first investigation.
clinicalPearl: In any adult with a lateral neck mass lasting more than 3 weeks, ultrasound-guided FNAC is the first investigation — open biopsy is always the last resort.
keyClue: 6 weeks duration, firm, non-tender, lateral, heavy smoker — malignancy until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CT scan as the first-line investigation; CT is important for staging but follows tissue diagnosis by FNAC.
missedConcept: FNAC alone has a 3-10% false-negative rate, so a negative result in a suspicious lump must be followed up — it does not exclude malignancy.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a soft, non-tender midline neck swelling that has been present since childhood. It moves upward on tongue protrusion and on swallowing. There is no fever or weight loss. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: A midline neck swelling present since childhood that moves upward on tongue protrusion is the classic description of a thyroglossal duct cyst. The neck mass lecture teaches the Rule of 7: a swelling present for years is most likely congenital. The upward movement on tongue protrusion is pathognomonic because the thyroglossal duct tract is attached to the foramen caecum of the tongue via a fibrous cord. Branchial cysts are lateral, typically appearing in the anterior triangle below the angle of the mandible in young adults. Reactive lymphadenopathy would be tender and often associated with infection. A thyroid nodule moves on swallowing but does not move upward on tongue protrusion. A dermoid cyst is midline but does not move on tongue protrusion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Thyroglossal duct cyst is the classic midline congenital neck swelling that moves upward on tongue protrusion — pathognomonic feature due to its fibrous tract to the foramen caecum.
Incorrect: Branchial cysts are lateral (anterior triangle, anterior to the sternocleidomastoid), not midline, and do not move on tongue protrusion.
Incorrect: Reactive lymphadenopathy is usually tender, associated with infection or inflammation, and does not demonstrate the characteristic movement on tongue protrusion.
Incorrect: A thyroid nodule moves on swallowing due to its investment in the pretracheal fascia, but does not move upward on tongue protrusion.
Incorrect: A dermoid cyst is midline but does not have a tract to the tongue base and therefore does not move on tongue protrusion.
clinicalPearl: Upward movement on tongue protrusion is pathognomonic of a thyroglossal duct cyst — no other neck mass does this.
keyClue: Midline + present since childhood + moves on tongue protrusion = thyroglossal duct cyst.
commonExamTrap: Confusing movement on swallowing (thyroid) with movement on tongue protrusion (thyroglossal cyst).
missedConcept: The Rule of 7 from the neck mass lecture: days = inflammatory, months = neoplastic, years = congenital.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 2.5 cm hard right level II cervical lymph node. FNAC confirms metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy, laryngoscopy, and examination under anaesthesia with biopsies of the nasopharynx, tongue base, and pyriform fossae are all negative. CT neck and chest shows no obvious primary. What is the MOST appropriate next investigation?
options: Core biopsy of the neck node
Open biopsy of the neck node
explanation: When a patient presents with metastatic SCC in the neck and no primary has been identified after full clinical examination, flexible laryngoscopy, and panendoscopy with directed biopsies, PET-CT is the investigation of choice according to the neck mass lecture. PET-CT detects metabolically active disease and can identify an occult primary tumour in the head and neck, lungs, or oesophagus that was not apparent on conventional imaging. It changes management in a significant proportion of patients with unknown primary disease. MRI provides superior soft tissue detail but is not sensitive enough to detect small occult primaries. Repeat FNAC will not add information as histology is already established. Core biopsy is indicated for lymphoma or salivary gland tumours, not when SCC is already confirmed. Open biopsy is the last resort and risks compromising subsequent surgical flaps.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: PET-CT is the recommended next step when panendoscopy fails to identify the primary in confirmed metastatic SCC of the neck — it detects occult metabolically active primaries not seen on conventional imaging.
Incorrect: MRI provides excellent soft tissue detail for local staging but has limited sensitivity for detecting small occult primary tumours compared to PET-CT in this clinical context.
Incorrect: Repeat FNAC is not indicated as the diagnosis of metastatic SCC is already confirmed; it would not identify the primary site.
Incorrect: Core biopsy is used when lymphoma or salivary gland malignancy is suspected and FNAC is non-diagnostic — the histological diagnosis of SCC is already established here.

Incorrect: Open biopsy is the investigation of absolute last resort; it should not be performed when tissue diagnosis is already confirmed, as it risks seeding and compromising future surgical options.
clinicalPearl: In confirmed metastatic SCC of the neck with negative panendoscopy, PET-CT is the standard next investigation to identify an occult primary.
keyClue: Metastatic SCC confirmed + panendoscopy negative + CT negative = PET-CT next.
commonExamTrap: Proceeding to open biopsy when histology is already known from FNAC — open biopsy is the last resort and should not be chosen when diagnosis is established.
missedConcept: PET-CT detects metabolically active disease not visible on conventional imaging and is the key investigation in unknown primary head and neck SCC.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old man originally from southern China presents with a 3-month history of a painless bilateral neck mass. He also reports left-sided nasal obstruction and an episode of blood-stained nasal discharge. Nasopharyngoscopy reveals a mass in the fossa of Rosenmuller. EBV serology is strongly positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: This presentation is classic for nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC). According to the Pharynx Tumors lecture, NPC is endemic in Southeast Asia and southern China, is strongly associated with EBV infection (and HPV), and the most common presentation is cervical lymphadenopathy (60% of cases). The fossa of Rosenmuller is the most common primary site. Other features include nasal obstruction (20%), conductive hearing loss/otitis media (20%), and cranial nerve palsies (10%) from skull base invasion. SCC accounts for 85% of NPC histology. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma can present with bilateral neck nodes but does not typically arise from the fossa of Rosenmuller and is not EBV-positive in this pattern. Branchial cysts are lateral benign congenital structures. Hypopharyngeal carcinoma presents with dysphagia and pain, not nasal symptoms. A parapharyngeal tumour would be lateral and not in the nasopharynx.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: NPC is the classic diagnosis — endemic in southern China, EBV-associated, presents with bilateral cervical LAP as the most common feature (60%), with the fossa of Rosenmuller as the primary site.
Incorrect: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma can cause bilateral neck nodes but does not originate from the fossa of Rosenmuller and would not show this pattern of EBV positivity combined with nasal symptoms.
Incorrect: Branchial cysts are benign congenital lateral neck swellings occurring in young adults; they are not associated with a nasopharyngeal mass or EBV serology.
Incorrect: Hypopharyngeal carcinoma arises from the piriform sinuses or post-cricoid region and presents primarily with dysphagia and referred otalgia, not bilateral cervical LAP and nasal symptoms.
Incorrect: Parapharyngeal space tumours typically present as unilateral bulging of the lateral pharyngeal wall or neck mass; they do not arise from the fossa of Rosenmuller.
clinicalPearl: NPC in an Asian male with bilateral cervical LAP + EBV positivity + fossa of Rosenmuller lesion = classic presentation; cervical LAP is the most common initial symptom in 60% of cases.
keyClue: Southern Chinese origin + EBV positivity + bilateral neck nodes + fossa of Rosenmuller mass.
commonExamTrap: Assuming lymphoma because of bilateral neck nodes — the nasopharyngeal mass and EBV association points firmly to NPC.
missedConcept: NPC is associated with both EBV (85% SCC in endemic areas) and dietary carcinogens (salted fish/nitrosamines); the most common presenting symptom is cervical lymphadenopathy, NOT nasal obstruction.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman presents with progressive dysphagia to solids, weight loss, and iron-deficiency anaemia. On examination she has angular cheilitis, glossitis, koilonychia, and a smooth tongue. Barium swallow shows a post-cricoid oesophageal web. What is the MOST likely underlying syndrome predisposing her to malignancy?
options: Paterson Brown Kelly syndrome (Plummer Vinson syndrome)
explanation: This is a classic description of Paterson Brown Kelly syndrome (also known as Plummer Vinson syndrome), which is covered in the Pharynx Tumors lecture. The syndrome consists of iron-deficiency anaemia, oesophageal/post-cricoid web, glossitis, koilonychia, and splenomegaly, occurring predominantly in middle-aged women. It is strongly associated with post-cricoid carcinoma, which is the only head and neck squamous cell carcinoma that is more common in women than in men. Treatment of the underlying iron deficiency may lead to regression of the web. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome predisposes to GI polyps and mucocutaneous pigmentation. Gardner syndrome predisposes to colonic polyposis and soft tissue tumours. Fanconi anaemia predisposes to haematological malignancies. Osler-Weber-Rendu involves hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Paterson Brown Kelly (Plummer Vinson) syndrome — iron-deficiency anaemia + post-cricoid web + glossitis + koilonychia + splenomegaly in a middle-aged woman, predisposing to post-cricoid SCC.
Incorrect: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is characterised by hamartomatous GI polyps and perioral mucocutaneous pigmentation; it does not cause iron-deficiency features or post-cricoid webs.
Incorrect: Gardner syndrome is a variant of FAP with colonic polyposis, desmoid tumours, and osteomas; it is not associated with the described clinical features.
Incorrect: Fanconi anaemia is a hereditary bone marrow failure syndrome predisposing to leukaemia and solid tumours, but it presents in childhood and does not cause post-cricoid webs or koilonychia.

Incorrect: Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome causes hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia with epistaxis and mucocutaneous telangiectases; it is not associated with iron-deficiency features or oesophageal webs.
clinicalPearl: Post-cricoid carcinoma is the ONLY head and neck SCC more common in women — always suspect in a woman with Paterson Brown Kelly syndrome.
keyClue: Iron-deficiency anaemia + post-cricoid web + koilonychia + glossitis in a woman = Paterson Brown Kelly syndrome → risk of post-cricoid SCC.
commonExamTrap: Missing the sex-specific epidemiology — post-cricoid SCC in women with iron deficiency is the classic exam scenario for Plummer Vinson syndrome.
missedConcept: Correction of iron deficiency may cause regression of the oesophageal web and could reduce malignant transformation risk.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man with a 3-month history of progressive dysphagia, hoarseness, and right-sided otalgia is seen in the emergency department with acute stridor and increasing difficulty breathing. Flexible laryngoscopy shows a large piriform sinus tumour with supraglottic extension and near-complete airway obstruction. His oxygen saturation is 88% on high-flow oxygen. What is the MOST important immediate management step?
options: Urgent CT scan of the neck and chest
explanation: This patient has acute airway compromise secondary to an advanced hypopharyngeal carcinoma with near-complete obstruction. Oxygen saturation of 88% despite high-flow oxygen signifies critical hypoxia. Emergency tracheostomy is the immediate life-saving intervention to secure the airway. The Pharynx Tumors lecture lists tracheostomy as a specific management option for hypopharyngeal carcinoma with airway involvement. Dexamethasone may temporarily reduce tumour oedema but is insufficiently fast-acting in critical obstruction with an SpO2 of 88%. CT scan is not appropriate as the first priority when the airway is critically compromised — secure the airway first. Radiotherapy takes days-to-weeks to shrink the tumour and cannot address an acute crisis. Rigid oesophagoscopy is a diagnostic procedure and risks worsening the obstruction in this acute setting.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Emergency tracheostomy is the immediate priority to secure the airway in a critically hypoxic patient with near-complete obstruction from advanced hypopharyngeal carcinoma — airway first.

Incorrect: IV dexamethasone may reduce peri-tumour oedema modestly but acts too slowly to reverse critical airway obstruction in a patient already desaturating to 88%; it is not the immediate intervention.
Incorrect: CT scan is essential for staging but must be deferred until the airway is secured; sending a critically hypoxic patient to CT is dangerous.
Incorrect: Radiotherapy is a definitive treatment option for hypopharyngeal carcinoma but requires days to weeks to have any effect on tumour size; it cannot address acute airway compromise.
Incorrect: Rigid oesophagoscopy for biopsy is an elective diagnostic procedure and would be contraindicated in this setting of critical airway obstruction.
clinicalPearl: In any head and neck cancer patient with stridor and critical hypoxia, securing the airway with tracheostomy takes absolute precedence over all investigations and definitive treatments.
keyClue: SpO2 88% + stridor + large piriform sinus tumour = acute airway emergency requiring tracheostomy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CT scan or steroids as the immediate step — airway always comes before imaging in acute obstruction.
missedConcept: Hypopharyngeal carcinoma (piriform sinus most common subsite) has a 5-year survival of only 35% and frequently presents late with airway compromise requiring tracheostomy.
redFlagReason: Acute airway compromise with SpO2 88% — failure to immediately secure the airway is potentially fatal.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with 8 weeks of progressive hoarseness. He denies dysphagia, otalgia, or neck mass. On flexible laryngoscopy an irregular friable lesion is seen on the free margin of the right anterior true vocal cord; the cord moves normally. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Microlaryngoscopy and biopsy under general anaesthesia
Voice therapy and reassessment in 3 months
High-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy for 6 weeks
explanation: The larynx lecture states that patients with hoarseness of more than 2 weeks should undergo laryngoscopy, and that biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis of laryngeal lesions — it provides histological confirmation essential for treatment planning. The irregular friable lesion on the free margin of the anterior true vocal cord in a heavy smoker is highly suspicious for glottic SCC (the most common subsite, accounting for 40-60% of laryngeal cancers). Microlaryngoscopy under GA allows direct visualisation, adequate biopsy sampling, and assessment of subglottic and supraglottic extension. The lecture warns that an insufficient biopsy results in sampling error. CT neck is used for staging but follows histological diagnosis. Voice therapy is inappropriate when malignancy is suspected. PPI therapy is relevant only if GERD-related laryngitis is suspected without a suspicious lesion. Chest X-ray alone does not address the primary diagnostic requirement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Microlaryngoscopy and biopsy under GA is the gold standard next step — it provides histological confirmation, allows full assessment of the lesion extent, and guides staging and treatment decisions.
Incorrect: CT neck is essential for staging (cartilage invasion, nodal disease) but should be performed after histological diagnosis is confirmed; it cannot replace biopsy.
Incorrect: Voice therapy is not appropriate when an irregular friable lesion suspicious for carcinoma is seen on laryngoscopy; delaying diagnosis risks progression of curable early-stage disease.

Incorrect: PPI therapy for presumed GERD is inappropriate here — an 8-week history of hoarseness with a visible friable lesion in a heavy smoker demands histological diagnosis, not empirical reflux treatment.

Incorrect: Chest X-ray may be part of the staging workup but is not the next step — tissue diagnosis comes first, as it determines whether staging investigations are required and guides treatment planning.
clinicalPearl: Any hoarseness lasting more than 2 weeks in a smoker requires laryngoscopy; a friable vocal cord lesion must be biopsied — biopsy is the gold standard for laryngeal cancer diagnosis.
keyClue: 8 weeks hoarseness + heavy smoker + friable lesion on true vocal cord = biopsy urgently.
commonExamTrap: Choosing CT scan before biopsy — CT is for staging, biopsy is for diagnosis. Diagnosis must precede staging.
missedConcept: Glottic SCC (true vocal cords) is the most common laryngeal cancer subsite (40-60%); the anterior portion of the free margin is the most frequent location.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive hoarseness and mild dysphagia. Flexible laryngoscopy shows a tumour involving the right true vocal cord with impaired but not fixed cord mobility; CT confirms extension into the supraglottis but no cartilage invasion and no lymphadenopathy. According to TNM staging for glottic carcinoma, what is the T-stage?
explanation: According to the larynx lecture glottic T-staging: T1 is tumour limited to the vocal cords with normal mobility (T1a = one cord, T1b = both cords). T2 is tumour that extends to the supraglottis and/or subglottis OR impairs vocal cord mobility. T3 is tumour limited to the larynx with vocal cord fixation. T4a involves invasion through the thyroid cartilage or beyond the larynx. This patient has a glottic tumour with extension to the supraglottis AND impaired (not fixed) cord mobility — both features independently qualify as T2. There is no cartilage invasion, so T4a is excluded. Vocal cord mobility is impaired but not fixed, excluding T3.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: T2 glottic SCC — defined as tumour extending to supraglottis and/or subglottis, or causing impaired (not fixed) vocal cord mobility; this patient has both supraglottic extension and impaired mobility.
Incorrect: T1b is bilateral true vocal cord involvement with normal cord mobility — this patient has supraglottic extension and impaired mobility, which exceeds T1 criteria.
Incorrect: T3 requires vocal cord fixation (not merely impairment); this patient has impaired but mobile cords, which defines T2, not T3.
Incorrect: T4a requires invasion through the thyroid cartilage and/or extension beyond the larynx — CT confirms no cartilage invasion in this patient.
Incorrect: T1a is tumour limited to one vocal cord with normal mobility — this patient has supraglottic extension and impaired cord mobility, both of which upgrade the stage to T2.
clinicalPearl: The key distinction for glottic staging: T2 = impaired mobility or supraglottic/subglottic extension; T3 = fixed cord; T4a = through cartilage or beyond larynx.
keyClue: Supraglottic extension + impaired (not fixed) cord mobility, no cartilage invasion = T2 glottic SCC.
commonExamTrap: Confusing impaired mobility (T2) with fixed cord (T3) — fixation requires complete immobility of the cord, not just impairment.
missedConcept: T2 can be reached by supraglottic/subglottic extension alone OR by impaired mobility alone — either criterion is sufficient.

clinicalStem: A 60-year-old man completes radical radiotherapy for T2N0M0 glottic carcinoma. Six months later he complains of persistent dry mouth, difficulty swallowing, and fatigue. Blood tests reveal a TSH of 12 mU/L with a low free T4. Which complication of radiotherapy does this BEST represent?
explanation: The larynx lecture lists the complications of radiotherapy (XRT) as: mucositis, odynophagia, laryngeal oedema, xerostomia, stricture and fibrosis, radionecrosis, and hypothyroidism. The biochemical finding of elevated TSH with low free T4 confirms primary hypothyroidism. The lecture specifically states that thyroid function tests should be checked every 6-12 months after larynx cancer treatment involving RT or extensive laryngeal/thyroid surgery because thyroid function is frequently reduced. Radiation to the neck damages the thyroid gland, leading to post-radiation hypothyroidism. Radionecrosis presents as pain, exposed cartilage, and tissue breakdown — not with TSH abnormality. Stricture and fibrosis cause dysphagia through luminal narrowing. Mucositis is an acute complication during treatment. Laryngeal oedema is usually an early post-treatment effect.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Hypothyroidism is a recognised long-term complication of neck irradiation; elevated TSH with low free T4 confirms primary hypothyroidism. NCCN guidelines mandate thyroid function monitoring every 6-12 months post-RT.
Incorrect: Radionecrosis presents with pain, exposed cartilage, perichondritis, and tissue breakdown — not with TSH abnormality or biochemical hypothyroidism.
Incorrect: Stricture and fibrosis is a late complication causing progressive dysphagia through fibrous narrowing of the pharynx or oesophagus; it does not cause biochemical thyroid abnormality.
Incorrect: Mucositis is an acute complication occurring during radiotherapy treatment, presenting with painful oral/pharyngeal mucosal ulceration; it resolves after treatment ends.
Incorrect: Laryngeal oedema is an early post-RT complication causing hoarseness and airway narrowing; it does not cause biochemical hypothyroidism.
clinicalPearl: Thyroid function tests must be checked every 6-12 months after neck radiotherapy — post-radiation hypothyroidism is a common, easily treatable late complication.
keyClue: Elevated TSH + low free T4 after neck RT = post-radiation primary hypothyroidism.
commonExamTrap: Attributing fatigue and swallowing difficulty only to radiation fibrosis and missing the biochemical diagnosis of hypothyroidism, which is a distinct and treatable complication.
missedConcept: Neck radiotherapy damages the thyroid gland leading to primary hypothyroidism; this is a long-term complication requiring routine biochemical surveillance post-treatment.

clinicalStem: A 15-year-old boy presents with a 4-month history of progressive unilateral nasal obstruction and three episodes of severe spontaneous epistaxis. On nasopharyngoscopy a purple, firm vascular mass is seen in the nasopharynx. CT scan shows a hypervascular mass centred around the sphenopalatine foramen with anterior bowing of the posterior wall of the maxillary sinus. Which is the MOST appropriate next step in management?
options: CT and MRI for staging, then pre-operative embolization followed by surgical resection
Intranasal steroid spray and watchful waiting
explanation: This is classic juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA). The SN Tumors lecture describes JNA as a rare but locally aggressive vascular tumour occurring almost exclusively in teenage boys, arising around the sphenopalatine foramen and presenting with progressive nasal obstruction and epistaxis. The CT finding of anterior bowing of the posterior maxillary wall is the Holman Miller sign — pathognomonic for JNA. The lecture specifically states that biopsy should be AVOIDED because the diagnosis can be made radiologically and severe haemorrhage may be encountered. Management involves CT and MRI for full staging (intracranial involvement in 10-20%), followed by pre-operative embolization within 24 hours of planned surgery to reduce vascularity and tumour bulk, then surgical resection (endoscopic or open). Radiotherapy is reserved for recurrent or unresectable cases. Watchful waiting is inappropriate for a growing vascular tumour causing severe epistaxis. Immediate surgery without embolization risks catastrophic haemorrhage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: The correct management sequence for JNA is: radiological diagnosis (CT + MRI for staging) → pre-operative embolization within 24 hours of surgery → surgical resection; biopsy must be avoided due to haemorrhage risk.
Incorrect: Biopsy must be AVOIDED in JNA — the diagnosis is radiological (Holman Miller sign on CT), and biopsy carries a high risk of uncontrollable haemorrhage from this highly vascular tumour.
Incorrect: Radiotherapy is not first-line treatment for JNA; surgery is the primary treatment. Radiotherapy may be considered for unresectable or recurrent disease.

Incorrect: Intranasal steroids are used for inflammatory nasal polyposis, not for a vascular neoplasm causing recurrent severe epistaxis; watchful waiting risks further haemorrhage and disease progression.
Incorrect: Immediate surgery without pre-operative embolization risks massive intraoperative haemorrhage; embolization within 24 hours of surgery significantly reduces blood loss.
clinicalPearl: In JNA, NEVER biopsy — the diagnosis is radiological. The Holman Miller sign (anterior bowing of posterior maxillary wall on CT) is pathognomonic. Embolize within 24 hours before surgery.
keyClue: Teenage boy + recurrent epistaxis + purple nasopharyngeal mass + Holman Miller sign on CT = JNA — diagnose radiologically, do not biopsy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing biopsy as the next step — this is specifically contraindicated in JNA due to catastrophic haemorrhage risk.
missedConcept: JNA can involve the skull base with intracranial extension in 10-20% of cases; MRI is needed to assess this. Pre-operative embolization must occur within 24 hours of planned surgery to prevent revascularisation.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man presents with 6 months of unilateral left nasal blockage, blood-stained rhinorrhoea, and reduced sense of smell. Nasal endoscopy reveals an irregular fleshy mass arising from the lateral nasal wall near the middle turbinate. CT scan shows a soft tissue mass in the left nasal cavity with erosion of the medial maxillary wall. Biopsy confirms an inverted papilloma. Which statement about this condition is MOST accurate?
options: Malignant transformation occurs in approximately 10% of cases, particularly in recurrent or bilateral lesions
It is most commonly bilateral and is strongly associated with cigarette smoking
Radiotherapy is the treatment of choice
FNAC is the preferred method for diagnosis
The most common site of origin is the nasal septum
explanation: The SN Tumors lecture states that inverted papilloma (Schneiderian papilloma) is a benign but locally aggressive tumour of the sinonasal mucosa. Malignant transformation occurs in approximately 10% of cases, particularly in recurrent and bilateral disease. The condition is associated with HPV types 6, 11, 16, and 18 — HPV types 16 and 18 are most likely responsible for malignant transformation. It occurs more frequently in men (3:1 ratio) at 1 per 100,000. Although benign, it behaves in a locally aggressive manner requiring wide local excision to prevent recurrence. The most common site is the lateral nasal wall around the middle turbinate, not the septum. Radiotherapy is not the treatment of choice — surgery (endoscopic or open) is. FNAC is not used for intranasal masses; endoscopic biopsy is required. The condition is not typically bilateral.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Malignant transformation in inverted papilloma is approximately 10%, especially in recurrent and bilateral cases; HPV 16 and 18 are most likely responsible for this transformation.

Incorrect: Inverted papilloma occurs more frequently in men (3:1) but is not typically bilateral; bilateral disease is actually a risk factor for malignant transformation, not a common characteristic.
Incorrect: Surgery (endoscopic or open/external approach) is the treatment of choice for inverted papilloma; radiotherapy has no primary role in its management.
Incorrect: FNAC is not appropriate for intranasal masses; histological diagnosis requires an endoscopic biopsy. FNAC is used for neck lumps, not sinonasal mucosal lesions.
Incorrect: The most common site of origin is the lateral nasal wall around the middle turbinate — not the nasal septum.
clinicalPearl: Inverted papilloma: benign but locally aggressive, lateral nasal wall origin, 10% malignant transformation (especially in recurrent/bilateral cases), HPV 16/18 implicated — treatment is wide surgical excision.
keyClue: Unilateral fleshy lateral nasal wall mass + irregular endoscopic appearance + biopsy confirming inverted papilloma = risk of malignant transformation (10%), especially if recurrent.
commonExamTrap: Assuming inverted papilloma never transforms malignantly — the 10% transformation rate (higher in recurrence) is a key exam fact.
missedConcept: HPV types 16 and 18 (high-risk types) are most associated with malignant transformation in inverted papilloma; HPV 6 and 11 (low-risk types) are associated with its formation but not transformation.

topic: Malignant tumours of the external nose
clinicalStem: A 64-year-old fair-skinned man presents with a 1.5 cm pearly, translucent, rolled-edge nodular lesion on the tip of his nose with central ulceration. He has had extensive sun exposure throughout his life. Biopsy confirms basal cell carcinoma. There is no lymphadenopathy. What is the MOST appropriate treatment?
options: Surgical excision, neck dissection, and post-operative radiotherapy
explanation: The SN Tumors lecture states that basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common malignant tumour of the external nose (75%), is caused by UV radiation, affects the basal layer of the epidermis, is locally invasive but does not metastasise to lymph nodes, and is treated by surgical excision with reconstruction at the same time. BCC is slower-growing than SCC. Because BCC does not typically metastasize to lymph nodes, neck dissection and radiotherapy are not part of standard management for localised disease. SCC of the external nose (20%) does metastasize to lymph nodes and requires excision + neck dissection + radiotherapy. Topical 5-FU can be used for superficial BCC but is not appropriate for a 1.5 cm nodular BCC with central ulceration. Cryotherapy is suitable only for small, superficial, non-infiltrative lesions and is not first-line for a nodular BCC on the nasal tip.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Surgical excision with immediate reconstruction is the treatment of choice for BCC of the external nose; BCC is locally invasive only and does not require neck dissection or adjuvant radiotherapy for localised disease.
Incorrect: Neck dissection and post-operative radiotherapy are indicated for SCC of the external nose (which metastasises to lymph nodes), not for BCC, which rarely if ever metastasises.

Incorrect: Radiotherapy alone is not the treatment of choice for BCC; surgery with reconstruction is preferred, particularly on the nose where cosmetic outcome is important and adequate margins can be achieved.
Incorrect: Topical 5-fluorouracil is used for superficial (not nodular) BCC and actinic keratoses; it is not appropriate for a 1.5 cm nodular ulcerating lesion on the nasal tip.

Incorrect: Cryotherapy is appropriate only for small, superficial, non-aggressive BCCs in low-risk locations; a 1.5 cm ulcerating nodular BCC on the nasal tip requires surgical excision with clear margins.
clinicalPearl: BCC = most common skin malignancy (75%) — locally invasive, NO lymph node metastasis, treated by excision + reconstruction. SCC = 20%, DOES metastasize, requires excision + neck dissection + radiotherapy.
keyClue: Pearly rolled-edge lesion + UV exposure + no lymphadenopathy + BCC on biopsy = surgical excision with reconstruction, no neck dissection needed.
commonExamTrap: Adding neck dissection to BCC management — neck dissection is for SCC (which metastasises), not BCC (which is locally invasive only).
missedConcept: The key distinction: BCC is slower-growing and does not metastasise to lymph nodes; SCC is faster-growing, covers a larger surface, bleeds easily on minimal trauma, and does metastasise — requiring neck dissection and radiotherapy in its management.
